sentence: Subjective:

HPI: The patient, a 20-year-old female, presents with a history of chronic asthma symptoms, which include coughing and wheezing that occur almost daily, with frequent exacerbations, particularly when she gets sick with a cold.
She reports that her symptoms worsen with exposure to her aunt's cat.
She has been experiencing these symptoms for the past six months, with frequent visits to urgent care and emergency department for treatment.
She has been prescribed medications, including steroids and antibiotics, and has been using an albuterol inhaler (Ventolin) daily.
She reports using two puffs of the inhaler almost every day, with occasional nighttime use.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of asthma, for which she has been using an albuterol inhaler (Ventolin) daily.
She has had one hospitalization for asthma at the age of 8 years, but none since then.
She has not had any other significant medical conditions or allergies.
Review of systems: The patient reports no other symptoms, except for her asthma symptoms.
Current medications: Albuterol inhaler (Ventolin) 2 puffs every 4-6 hours as needed for rescue; Flovent 110 micrograms 1 puff twice daily for inhaled steroid.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 128/82 mmHg, respiratory rate 16, oxygen saturation 99% on room air.
Physical exam: The patient has diminished lung sounds with occasional slight expiratory wheeze, bilaterally.
Neck is supple, trachea is midline, no lymphadenopathy, no carotid bruit.
Strong bilateral pulses, no clubbing, brisk capillary refill.
Test Results: Pulmonary function test results consistent with asthma.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has moderate intermittent asthma.
Problem list: Asthma; Asthma (status: active).
Plan:
I prescribed Flovent 110 micrograms 1 puff twice daily for inhaled steroid to help prevent daily symptoms and reduce the need for rescue medication.
I instructed the patient to continue using her albuterol inhaler (Ventolin) 2 puffs every 4-6 hours as needed for rescue.
I also created an asthma action plan to help manage her symptoms and identify triggers.
Follow-up:
I plan to see the patient in 30 days to review her progress and adjust her treatment plan as needed.
